prevalence.trend: Increasing due to urbanization and climate change

incidence.source: Brazilian Society of Allergology and Immunology (ASBAI)

burden.source: Global Burden of Disease Study (IHME, adapted for Brazil)

riskFactors: Exposure to environmental allergens (dust mites, pollen, mold, animal dander)
Air pollution and urban environmental factors
vulnerablePopulations: Children and adolescents (prevalence up to 40% in urban areas)
Urban residents in polluted cities like São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro
Individuals with atopic dermatitis or family history of allergies
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Based on ARIA guidelines: Persistent or intermittent symptoms including sneezing, nasal itching, rhinorrhea, and congestion triggered by allergens; confirmed by skin prick tests or serum IgE levels

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: Low (estimated 20-30% in primary care via SUS)
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Approximately 53 million (based on 25% prevalence in 214 million population)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Around 60% with access to treatment, lower in rural areas

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Adapted ARIA criteria with emphasis on local allergens like house dust mites prevalent in humid tropical climate
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: SUS protocol includes intranasal corticosteroids, oral antihistamines, and allergen immunotherapy; guidelines from ASBAI
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: High urban pollution exacerbating symptoms; limited access in remote Amazon regions; year-round allergens due to tropical environment
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Universal coverage via SUS for basic treatments; strong research from institutions like Fiocruz; growing awareness campaigns
